pattern: Fix the target count of ones first, shrink to the minimum window length that can hold exactly that many, then compare every window of that shortest length as a plain string to pick the smallest one.
hints: Think about the two requirements separately: first pin down how short a qualifying window can possibly be, then only compare among windows of that exact length.
A window is only interesting if it contains precisely k ones -- track a running count of ones as you slide the window's edges and discard anything with too many or too few.
Once you know the minimum length, check every window of that length, keep the ones with exactly k ones, and compare those substrings the normal way (character by character) to keep the smallest.

edge_inputs.description: no ones at all, no beautiful substring exists
single character that is itself beautiful
several equal-length candidates that are identical
maximum length string, all ones, k equal to the length
the single beautiful character sits at the very end of a long run of zeros
multiple windows share the shortest length and only a true lexicographic compare finds the smallest
a single one gives a shorter beautiful substring than any run of several ones would
ones are clustered together so the shortest beautiful substring is exactly that cluster

stress.description: random binary strings up to the maximum length with a random achievable or unachievable k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(['0', '1']) for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [s, k]

complexity.time: O(n^3) worst case over window lengths and comparisons
